document-meta.document-title: Polymer Brush-Grafted Silicon Nanoparticles as a Binder-Free Anode for Enhanced Lithium-Ion Battery Performance

contrib-group.aff: †Fluoro and Agrochemicals Division, CSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Technology, Hyderabad, 500007, India.
‡Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, Ghaziabad 201002, India.
¶Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Oregon, Eugene, OR 97403, United States
§Center for X-ray Crystallography, CSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Technology, Hyderabad, 500007, India.
ΔFluoro and Agrochemicals Division, CSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Technology, Hyderabad, 500007, India.

abstract.p: Silicon (Si) anodes offer a high theoretical energy capacity but face challenges due to significant volume changes during cycling, which lead to Si particle pulverization, electrode delamination, and ultimately poor cycle-life performance. This study introduces Si nanoparticles grafted with polymer brushes as a binder-free, high-performance lithium-ion battery anode material. Si nanoparticles are initially coated with poly{(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-co-3-[1-ethyl-2-(2-bromoisobutyrate)]thiophene} (P(EDOT-co-EBBT)), which acts as both an electrical conductor and a surface initiator for surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP). Through SI-ATRP, poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA) brushes are then grafted, forming an Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush structure. The chemical structure of the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush is characterized by infrared spectroscopy, while its core-shell structure is confirmed through scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy. The Si composite anode exhibits a high charge capacity of 2694 mAh g−1 at 0.2 C, enhanced C-rate performance, and good cycle-life stability. Rheological measurements indicate strong interactions between the PMAA brushes of Si nanoparticles, enabling the PMAA to function effectively as a binder. Peeling tests show improved adhesion properties for the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA composite electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy reveals reduced resistance, and cyclic voltammetry confirms high redox diffusion coefficient for the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush anodes.

abstract-group.synopsis: This approach, utilizing polymer-grafted Si nanoparticles as a binder-free anode, significantly enhances the performance of lithium-ion batteries and represents a promising advancement in anode material development.

processing-meta.x: Yu-Lung Chang − Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan
Chia-Chen Li − Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu 300044, Taiwan; orcid.org/ 0000-0001-9336-2972
Swetha V. Chaganti − Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan
Santosh U. Sharma − Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan
Sung-Lin Ho − Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan
Yan-Jhang Chen − Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan
Pin-Jyun Chen − Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan
Complete contact information is available at:

sec.head: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Traditional fossil fuels face challenges such as resource depletion, complex exploration processes, and environmental pollution. Researchers have been developing clean and renewable energy sources. Wind and solar energy are promising alternatives; however, they require efficient energy storage systems. Among these systems, lithium-ion batteries have emerged as a leading technology in energy conversion and storage. At the same time, increasing the energy density of lithium-ion batteries has become a focus for their application in electric vehicles and portable electronic devices. Therefore, researchers are working to develop high energy-density anodes and cathodes.

Over the past few decades, graphite has been widely used as a conventional anode material in lithium-ion batteries due to its high electrical conductivity and low average working voltage of 0.1 V vs. Li/Li+. However, the energy density of lithium-ion batteries is limited by the low theoretical capacity of graphite, which is only 372 mAh g−1. To address this limitation, various alternatives have been extensively studied. Among them, silicon (Si) is one of the most promising anode materials due to its high theoretical capacity of 4200 mAh g−1. Unfortunately, Si material is associated with significant volume changes and low intrinsic electrical conductivity during the lithiation/delithiation process. These factors ultimately result in the pulverization of Si particles and permanent capacity fading, hindering the practical use of Si in lithium-ion batteries. To overcome these challenges, numerous strategies have been employed, including the use of nano-sized Si, surface coating of Si with different conducting materials,– and the development of Si-based composites., Additional approaches to compensate for significant volume changes include fabricating Si core-shell structures with increased void space,– utilizing nanoporous Si materials to buffer volume changes, and employing appropriate binders to maintain electrode integrity and ensure continuous electrical conduction pathways. Recently, conducting polymers such as polypyrrole, polyaniline, polythiophene, and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) have been employed as coating layers on Si nanoparticles as composite electrodes, harnessing their electrical conductivity and inherent flexibility. These Si composite electrodes have demonstrated improved cell performance in lithium-ion batteries.– Additionally, introducing an electrically conductive shell layer to the surface of Si anode-active material has proven to be an effective approach for enhancing electrochemical performance.–
In our earlier studies on aminations refs – still highly desirable and warrants further explorations
In our earlier studies on aminations ref 16 17 18 19 still highly desirable and warrants further explorations

Due to the significant volume changes of Si during the lithiation/delithiation process, maintaining the integrity of Si composite electrodes is crucial for their cycle-life performance. Therefore, the binder used in Si composite electrodes plays a vital role. Various binders for Si anodes have been reported, including chitosan, sodium alginate, starch, β-cyclodextrin polymer, gums,– styrene-butadiene rubber/carboxymethylcellulose (SBR/CMC), polyurethane, conducting polymers, small molecules,, polyisoprene-graft-maleic anhydride, and poly(acrylic acid)., Most of binders for Si nanoparticles contain polar hydroxyl or carboxylic groups, which interact with hydroxyl groups on Si anodes through hydrogen bonding; however, this bonding may be insufficient to withstand the mechanical stress from the substantial volume change of Si anodes during the charge-discharge process. Compared to ionic, covalent, and metallic bonding, hydrogen bonding is relatively weak. Thus, enhancing the interaction between the binder and Si by incorporating covalent bonding is a promising approach.

In this study, to enhance the electrical conductivity of Si anodes, a layer of the conducting polymer is coated onto Si nanoparticles. Additionally, a poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA) brush is grafted onto the conducting polymer, acting as a binder to improve the adhesion of the Si-active material. We deposit a thin layer of poly{(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-co-3-[1-ethyl-2-(2-bromoisobutyrate)]thiophene}, P(EDOT-co-EBBT), onto Si nanoparticles through a reaction coating process. The P(EDOT-co-EBBT) layer is then grafted with PMAA brush using surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP), forming the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, as shown in Scheme . The PMAA brush, which serves as a binder, is covalently grafted onto the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT) nanoparticles, eliminating the need for an additional binder in the Si slurry. The dispersion of this binder-free Si slurry is evaluated using rheological measurements. The polymer brush acts as a binder by chemically binding to the Si anode, contributing to the excellent mechanical stability of the electrodes. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results show that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode exhibits lower resistance, leading to enhanced battery performance in terms of C-rate and cycle-life performance.

sec.head: Materials
sec.p: 3,4-Ethylenedioxythiophene (1) (EDOT, > 98%, TCI), iron(III) chloride (FeCl3, > 98%, Alfa), chloroform (99%, ECHO), methanol (99.5%, Macron), 3-thiopheneacetic acid (3) (ThAA, > 98%, Fluka), sulfuric acid (95-97%, Scharlau), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%, SHOWA), hydrochloric acid (HCl, ≥ 37%, Sigma-Aldrich), 2-(3-thienyl)ethanol (9) (99%, Acros), triethylamine (> 99.5%, Sigma-Aldrich), trifluoroacetic acid (98%, Alfa), 2-bromo-2-methylpropionyl bromide (10) (98%, Sigma-Aldrich), tert-butyl methacrylate (t-BMA, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich), dichloromethane (99.5%, Duksan), anhydrous magnesium sulfate (99%, Sigma-Aldrich), ethyl acetate (HPLC grade, ECHO), hexane (99%, Macron), ammonium hydroxide aqueous solution (28-38%, J.T. Baker), N,N,N’,N’,N’’-pentamethyldiethylenetriamine (PMDETA, 99%, Sigma-Aldrich), tetrahydrofuran (THF, HPLC grade, Duksan), copper(I) bromide (CuBr, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99%, Jassen), poly(vinylidenefluoride) (PVDF, KF-1100, Kureha), Super P (conductive carbon, Timcal), Si nanoparticles (an average particle size of 50 nm, Alfa Aesar), lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6, Novolyte Technologies), copper foil (UBIQ Technology, Co., Ltd.), polypropylene (PP) separator (Celgard 2500), ethylene carbonate (EC, Novolyte Technologies), diethyl carbonate (DEC, Novolyte Technologies), fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC, Novolyte Technologies), and lithium metal foil (UBIQ Technology, Co., Ltd.) were used without further purification or modification. The syntheses of PEDOT, poly(3-thiopheneacetic acid) (PThAA), P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA are shown in Supporting Information.

sec.head: Instrumentation
sec.p: On a PerkinElmer UATR for Spectrum Two L1600107, attenuated total reflectance infrared (ATR-IR) spectra were collected in the 400–4000 cm–1 frequency range. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded using a JEOL NMR 400 MHz spectrometer. Rheological properties of the electrode slurries were measured with a rheometer (MCR302e; Anton Paar, Graz, Austria). The dynamic frequency sweep test used a cone-plate geometry fixture with a 25 mm diameter and a 1° cone angle. The test was conducted within the linear viscoelastic regime, with an angular frequency ranging from 0.1 to 100 rad s–1 at a specified strain. Morphological analysis of Si-active materials was measured on transmission electron microscope (TEM) using JEM-3010 (JEOL, Japan) with energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDS, X-MAX80T). Surface morphologies of Si nanoparticles and Si composite electrodes were obtained by scanning electron microscope (SEM) on a SUPER 55-VP (Zeiss, Germany). Electrochemical performance was recorded on a CH instrument (model CHI-627D, USA). Galvanostatic charge-discharge tests were performed on a LAND battery testing system with a model CT20001. In addition, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and EIS analyses were carried out on a CH instrument (model CHI-750A, USA). Peeling tests were performed using a vertical electric stand (JSV-500D).

sec.head: Synthesis of Si@poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (Si@PEDOT)
sec.p: EDOT (1) (0.1 g, 0.7 mmol) was dissolved in chloroform (50 mL). Then, Si nanoparticles (0.5 g) were added to the chloroform solution and stirred for 10 min. Subsequently, a chloroform solution (10 mL) of FeCl3 (0.46 g, 2.8 mmol) was added dropwise to the above solution under a nitrogen atmosphere. The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the reaction mixture was added to methanol (200 mL) and stirred for 30 min. to obtain a precipitate. The precipitate was filtered, washed with methanol, and purified by Soxhlet extraction using methanol for 24 h. The resulting precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight to give Si@PEDOT nanoparticles, resulting in a yield of 76.8%.

sec.head: Synthesis of methyl 3-thiopheneacetate (MThA) (4)
sec.p: 3-Thiopheneacetic acid (3) (1.0 g, 7.0 mmol) was dissolved in methanol (40 mL), and one drop of concentrated sulfuric acid was added into the methanol solution. Then, the solution was refluxed for 24 h. After the reaction, the solution was concentrated under reduced pressure to remove methanol, and the residue was re-dissolved with diethyl ether (40 mL). The ether solution was extracted with deionized water (20 mL) three times, and the ether layer was taken and dried over anhydrous magnesium sulfate and filtered. Finally, the ether solution was concentrated by vacuum drying to give the product, MThA (4), a yield of 86.2%. 1H NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz): δ 7.29 (dd, 1H), 7.15 (dd, 1H), 3.71 (s, 1H), 3.67 (s, 9H). 13C NMR (CDCl3, 100 MHz): δ 171.7, 133.7, 128.6, 125.9, 123.02, 52.2, 35.8.

sec.head: Synthesis of Si@poly(methyl 3-thiopheneacetate) (Si@PMThA)
sec.p: MThA (4) (0.1 g, 0.6 mmol) and Si nanoparticles (0.5 g) were added in chloroform (50 mL), and then the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 10 min. Subsequently, under a nitrogen atmosphere, a chloroform solution (10 mL) of FeCl3 (0.38 g, 2.4 mmol) was added to the mixture, which was then stirred at room temperature for 24 h to undergo reaction coating. After the reaction coating, the reaction mixture was poured into methanol (200 mL) and stirring was continued for 30 min. to isolate a precipitate. The precipitate was filtered, washed with methanol, and purified by Soxhlet extraction using methanol for 24 h. Finally, the purified precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight, yielding Si@PMThA with a yield of 64.5%.

sec.head: Hydrolysis of Si@PMThA to Si@PThAA
sec.p: Si@PMThA (0.25 g) was added to a 2.0 M NaOH aqueous solution (20 mL), and then the mixture was heated at 100 °C with stirring for 24 h. Subsequently, the mixture was filtered, and the resulting solid was neutralized using a diluted aqueous HCl solution. A precipitate was then filtered, washed with deionized water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight to give Si@PThAA with a yield of 47.5%.

sec.head: Synthesis of Si@poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene-co-methyl 3-thiopheneacetate), Si@P(EDOT-co-MThA)
sec.p: EDOT (1) (0.10 g, 0.70 mmol), MThA (4) (0.11 g, 0.70 mmol), and Si nanoparticles (1.05 g) were added in chloroform (100 mL) and the chloroform solution was stirred at room temperature for 10 min. Subsequently, a chloroform solution (10 mL) of FeCl3 (0.92 g, 5.6 mmol) was added into the above chloroform solution under a nitrogen atmosphere. The solution was stirred at room temperature for 24 h to undergo polymerization. After polymerization, the mixture was poured to methanol (200 mL) and stirred for 30 min to precipitate the product, Si@P(EDOT-co-MThA). The precipitate was filtered and washed with methanol. Finally, the precipitate was purified by Soxhlet extraction using methanol for 24 h, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight, resulting in a yield of 51.3%.

sec.head: Hydrolysis of Si@P(EDOT-co-MThA) to Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA)
sec.p: Si@P(EDOT-co-MThA) (0.2 g) was heated in an aqueous 2.0 M NaOH solution (20 mL) at 100 °C with stirring for 24 h. After hydrolysis, the solution was neutralized using an aqueous HCl solution (1.0 M). A precipitate, Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), was then filtered and washed with deionized water. The precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight, yield 31.5%.

sec.head: Synthesis of 3-[1-ethyl-2(2-bromoisobutyrate)]thiophene (EBBT) (11)
sec.p: 2-(3-Thienyl)ethanol (9) (1.0 g, 7.8 mmol) and triethylamine (1.2 mL, 8.6 mmol) were dissolved in dichloromethane (15 mL) and cooled in an ice bath. Then, a dichloromethane solution (5 mL) of 2-bromo-2-methylpropionyl bromide (10) (1.06 mL, 8.6 mmol) was added in the above dichloromethane solution dropwise. After addition, the ice bath was removed and the solution was warm up to room temperature. The reaction was carried out under an atmosphere of nitrogen for 24 h. After reaction, the reaction solution was extracted with 10 mL of following solvents: 1 M dilute HCl, saturated aqueous sodium bicarbonate, saturated saline, and deionized water. Then, the dichloromethane layer was dried with anhydrous magnesium sulfate and filtered. The dichloromethane layer was further concentrated by rotavapor. The crude oil was purified by column chromatography using an eluent of ethyl acetate/n-hexane (= 1:19, v/v) to obtain EBBT (11), yield 50.4 %.

sec.head: Synthesis of Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)
sec.p: EBBT (11) (0.19 g, 0.7 mmol) and EDOT (1) (0.1 g, 0.7 mmol) monomers were dissolved in chloroform (100 mL). Afterwards, Si nanoparticles (1.45 g) was added to the solution, and stirred for 10 min. Subsequently, a chloroform solution (100 mL) of FeCl3 (0.92 g, 5.6 mmol) was added dropwise to the monomer solution under a nitrogen atmosphere and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. After the reaction, the reaction mixture was poured into methanol (400 mL) to precipitate the product and stirred for 30 min. Then, the precipitate was filtered, washed with methanol and purified by Soxhlet extraction with methanol for 24 h. Finally, the precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight, yielding Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT) in a yield of 61.8%.

sec.head: Synthesis of Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-P(t-BMA) Brush
sec.p: Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT) (0.6 g) and PMDETA (0.06 mL, 0.29 mmol) were dissolved in THF (70 mL). The THF solution was degassed by three freeze-pump-thaw cycles. CuBr (0.04 g, 0.28 mmol) was transferred to a reaction flask in a glovebox and added with the above THF solution using a double-ended needle. t-BMA (0.41 mL, 2.46 mmol) degassed by three freeze-pump-thaw cycles was transferred to the above THF solution using a double-ended needle. Then, the reaction mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. After reaction, the reaction mixture was added to n-hexane (400 mL) and stirred for 30 min to precipitate the product. The precipitate was filtered and washed with n-hexane and deionized water. After washing, the precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight to give Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-P(t-BMA) brush with a conversion of 55.6%.

sec.head: Hydrolysis of Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-P(t-BMA) Brush to Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA Brush
sec.p: Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-P(t-BMA) brush (0.2 g) was added to trifluoroacetic acid (20 mL) was added to the above reaction mixture. The reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. After reaction, the mixture was filtered to remove trifluoroacetic acid and dried under a vacuum to give Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush with a conversion of 89.2%.

sec.head: Fabrication of Electrodes
sec.p: The Si composite electrode was comprised of Si nanoparticles (Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), or Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA, 80 wt%) and Super P (20 wt%). For control electrode systems, Si composite electrodes were prepared by mixing Si nanoparticles, PVDF or conducting polymer (PEDOT, PThA, PEDOT-co-PThA, or PEDOT-co-PThA-g-MAA), and Super P in a weight ratio of 60:20:20. The ingredients were mixed and stirred in NMP (for the Si/PVDF electrode) or deionized water (2 mL) for 24 h to form a homogeneous slurry. The slurry was cast onto copper foil using a doctor blade and dried in vacuum oven for 12 h at 120 °C. After drying the electrodes were cut into circular pieces of 13 mm in diameter. The mass loading of the Si composite electrodes was 1.32-2.07 mg cm–2. The specific capacity of the Si-based anode is calculated based on the mass of the Si@polymer composite.

sec.head: Electrochemical Characterizations
sec.p: A CR2032 coin cell was assembled in an Ar-filled glovebox to measure electrochemical performance of Si composite electrodes. The assembled cell consists of anode, cathode, and separator as Si electrode, lithium foil, and PP membrane, respectively. The coin cells were assembled using 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC:DEC:FEC (45:45:10/w:w:w) as an electrolyte. CV was evaluated at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s–1 in the potential range of 0.01–3.0 V vs. Li/Li+. In addition, the cycle-life performance of the cells was evaluated at a charge-discharge rate of 0.5 C for 100 cycles. The charge-discharge rates used in the C-rate test were 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C. Furthermore, the AC impedance measurement was carried out in the frequency range of 0.1–100k Hz with a perturbation amplitude of 5 mV. All of the above electrochemical measurements were performed at 30 °C. Chart

sec.head: Adhesion Tests of Electrodes
sec.p: The 180° peeling tests of Si composite electrodes were performed on a vertical electric test instrument with a peeling rate of 2 cm min–1 (Figure S7). The electrodes were taped with 3M scotch tape (2 cm in width) and peeled vertically using 180° peeling machine.

sec.head: Syntheses of Si@conducting Polymer Core-Shell Materials
sec.p: In this study, four Si@conducting polymer core-shell materials were synthesized, such as Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush as shown in Scheme a-d, respectively. The Si@PEDOT core-shell material was synthesized by oxidatively polymerizing EDOT (1) on Si (Scheme a). For the synthesis of Si@PThAA, we have tried to polymerize ThAA (3) directly on the Si nanoparticles. However, the polymerization did not undergo, which may be due to the chelation of carboxylic acid of ThAA (3) with FeCl3. Therefore, the carboxylic acid of ThAA (3) was protected with a methyl group, yielding a protected monomer, MThA (4). Then, the MThA (4) monomer was oxidatively polymerized to deposit on the Si nanoparticles, forming Si@PMThA core-shell nanoparticles. Finally, the protecting groups of PMThA on Si were deprotected using a NaOH aqueous solution to form Si@PThAA core-shell nanoparticles (Scheme b). To synthesize Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), the monomers of EDOT (1) and MThA (4) were copolymerized on the Si nanoparticles, resulting in Si@P(EDOT-co-MThA) core-shell nanoparticles. These nanoparticles were then hydrolyzed in a NaOH solution to yield Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA) core-shell nanoparticles (Scheme c). For the synthesis of Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, the EDOT (1) and EBBT (11) monomers were polymerized on Si to form Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT). Furthermore, Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT) was grafted with t-BMA to obtain Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-P(t-BMA) brush using SI-ATRP. Finally, Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-P(t-BMA) brush was hydrolyzed by trifluoroacetic acid to produce Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush (Scheme d).

sec.head: Characterizations of Si@conducting Polymer Core-Shell Materials
sec.p: The chemical structures of the Si@conducting polymer core-shell materials were characterized by IR spectroscopy. Figure a(i), (ii), (iii), and (iv) show the IR spectra of Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, respectively. In Figure a(i), the IR spectrum of Si@PEDOT shows absorption bands at 1514 and 1302 cm–1, corresponding to the C=C and C-C stretching of the thiophene ring., Additionally, absorption bands at 1133 and 832 cm–1 are ascribed to C-O-C and C-S stretching of PEDOT. The IR spectrum of Si@PThA in Figure a(ii) exhibits an absorption band at 3500–2500 cm–1 attributed to the stretching vibration of the OH group of carboxylic acid, and two bands at 1709, 1199, and 876 cm–1 assigned to the C=O (carboxylic acid), C-O (carboxylic acid), and C-S (thiophene ring) stretching vibrations. The IR spectrum of Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA) in Figure a(iii) shows an absorption band of OH groups of carboxylic acid between 3500–2500 cm–1. Moreover, characteristic absorption bands at 1709, 1199, 1136, and 838 cm–1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C=O (carboxylic acid), C-O (carboxylic acid), C-O-C (PEDOT), and C-S (thiophene ring), respectively. For Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA (Figure a(iv)), two major absorption bands at 1692 and 1153 cm–1 are observed, attributed to C=O and C-O stretching of the PMAA brush. The absence of absorption bands at 1133 and 832 cm–1 for PEDOT indicates a high percentage of the PMAA brush. To confirm the characteristic absorption bands of the IR spectra for the Si@conducting polymer core-shell materials, free polymers of PEDOT, PThAA, P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA were synthesized and then characterized by IR spectroscopy. The IR spectra of the polymers are provided in Figure b(i)-(iv). The IR spectrum results of the free polymers closely resemble those of the Si@conducting polymer core-shell materials because Si is IR-inactive. Furthermore, the absence of distinct Si–O absorption bands (∼1050–1100 cm–1) for SiO2 suggests that the oxide layer on the Si nanoparticles may be very thick. The IR spectra results confirm the successful polymerization and SI-ATRP on Si nanoparticles.

TEM images were utilized to observe the core-shell structure of the Si nanoparticles. To assess the stability of the shell layers on the Si nanoparticles against solvents and electrolytes, all Si nanoparticles were cast as Si composite electrodes and then immersed in electrolyte. Then, the electrodes underwent water washing and ultrasonication in water. Figure a-e show the TEM images of Si, Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush anodes, respectively. In Figure a, the Si nanoparticles exhibit nothing on their surface. However, Figure b-d show a coating layer on the Si nanoparticles for Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, and Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), respectively. These thin layers on the Si nanoparticles suggest that the coating layers of conducting polymers can resist dissolution in the electrolyte. Furthermore, the TEM image of Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA (Figure e) shows a melted-like morphology on the surface of the Si nanoparticles, indicating that PMAA brushes were entangled. In addition, EDS analysis of various core-shell nanoparticles was performed (Figure S9). No S signal was detected for bare Si nanoparticles. However, all Si@conducting polymer nanoparticles exhibite an S signal originating from the polythiophene backbone, confirming the successful coating of conducting polymers on Si nanoparticles. Notably, the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush nanoparticles exhitbit a lower S percentage due to the higher percentage of PMAA brushes. Our previous publication also shows that nitroxide polymer brushes grafted on silica nanoparticles can serve as binder-free cathodes. Therefore, the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush is also applicable for binder-free Si anodes.

Furthermore, SEM were performed to investigate the surface morphology of the Si nanoparticles. Figure f–j show the SEM images for the Si, Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush nanoparticles, respectively. Figure f shows that the surface of the pristine Si nanoparticles is smooth because it does not be modified. Figure g–i show SEM images of surfaces of Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, and Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA) nanoparticles, respectively, indicating that a layers was coated on the Si nanoparticles. However, Figure j shows that the surface morphology of Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush is different from others. The Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush exhibits a melted-like morphology, which is due to the PMAA brushes being entangled together. Therefore, the SEM results also indicate that the PMAA brush may serve as a binder in the Si electrode.

sec.head: Rheological Behaviors of Si Core-Shell Nanoparticles
sec.p: The dispersity of Si nanoparticle aqueous slurries were studied by rheological measurements. It is known that the storage modulus (G’) value measures the energy stored and recovered during deformation, indicating the structural integrity or elasticity of the dispersed phase. It is affected by interactions between particles, flocculation, and the formation of networks within the dispersion medium. A higher G’ signifies stronger interparticle forces or increased structuring. On the other hand, the loss modulus (G") value represents the energy dissipated as heat due to internal friction when the slurry is deformed, which relates to its flowability. When G’ is greater than G", the slurry exhibits solid-like behavior, suggesting the presence of a well-structured network with significant particle interactions. Conversely, when G’ is less than G", the slurry behaves more like a liquid, indicating weak particle interactions or dominant viscous effects. When G’ and G" are approximately equal, it indicates a transition between solid-like and liquid-like behavior. In battery applications, particularly for electrode slurries, optimizing both G’ and G" is crucial for achieving a uniform and stable dispersion of electrode materials. In SEM image of Si@PEDOT (Figure g) shows that the Si@PEDOT nanoparticles resulted in significant agglomeration, which can be attributed to the lipophilicity of PEDOT. The surface coverage by PEDOT makes the Si@PEDOT nanoparticles chemically incompatible with the water-based slurry, leading to poor dispersion and agglomeration. This poor dispersion was confirmed by rheological measurements in Figure a, where the measured G’ and G" were frequency-independent, with G’ being larger than G", indicating solid-like flow behavior typically associated with particle agglomeration. Similarly, Si nanoparticles shelled with PThAA (Si@PThAA) also showed significant agglomeration, as seen in Figure h. However, the corresponding G’ and G" values in the prepared slurry in Figure b decreased slightly compared to those of the Si@PEDOT slurry, possibly due to PThAA carrying hydrophilic -COOH groups, which makes Si@PThAA less hydrophobic and improves its compatibility in the water-based slurry. When the Si nanoparticles were coated with P(EDOT-co-ThAA) to produce Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), the particle agglomeration was reduced, as shown in Figure i, and this improved dispersion is consistent with the further decreased values of G’ and G" measured in Figure c. The most significant improvement in particle dispersion was observed when Si nanoparticles were coated with the hydrophilic brush, P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA, resulting in Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA, as shown in the SEM image in Figure j. The rheological analysis in Figure d shows that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush slurry exhibits solid-like and elastic behavior at shorter observation times (higher frequencies) and transformed into gel-like behavior at longer observation times (frequencies < 1 rad s–1). Such rheological behavior is typically indicative of a slurry that has strong interactions, such as gelation or agglomeration, of its component materials. In this case, the SEM image in Figure j indicate that the strong interaction stemmed from observable connections between PMAA brushes of Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA nanoparticles rather than severe particle agglomeration.

sec.head: Electrochemical Performance
sec.p: Figure a–d show the first five CV curves of the Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrodes, respectively. The CV curves of the Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, and Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA) electrodes exhibit lower current densities compared to the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrodes, suggesting that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode may have a higher specific energy capacity or higher diffusion coefficient of lithium ions in the Si anodes. Additionally, the CV curve of the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode (Figure d) displays an irreversible broad peak in the range of 0.5–0.8V vs Li/Li+, corresponding to the formation of the solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) due to electrolyte degradation. Furthermore, two reduction peaks appear at 0.25 and 0.01 V, corresponding to the formation of Li-Si alloys. Two oxidation peaks at 0.32 and 0.50 V are attributed to the conversion of Li-Si alloys into amorphous Si., The redox peaks of the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode remain relatively stable throughout the cycling period, indicating the reversibility of the electrode during lithiation and delithiation processes.

Figure a-d show the charge-discharge curves of Si electrodes with Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrodes at different C-rates, respectively. The Si@PEDOT electrode shows charge capacities of 2527, 1938, 1679, and 1289 mAh g–1, whereas for Si@PThAA the capacities are 2561, 1591, 943, and 467 mAh g–1 at C-rates of 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C, respectively. The specific charge capacities of the Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA) electrode are 2672, 2403, 2058, and 1493 mAh g–1 at C-rates of 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C, respectively, which are higher than those of the Si@PEDOT and Si@PThAA electrodes. Figure d shows that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrode exhibits specific charge capacities of 2694, 2538, 2343, and 2134 mAh g–1 at C-rates of 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C, respectively. The Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrode exhibits better C-rate performance. This improvement may be due to the brush binder being covalently attached to the Si electrode, providing better homogeneity and stability. Therefore, the Si nanoparticles with the PMAA brush as a binder-free cathode can enhance the electrochemical performance of lithium-ion batteries.

Figure e(i)-(iv) show the cycle-life performance of Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrodes measured during the charge-discharge test within 0.01–1.2 V at a C-rate of 0.5 C, respectively. The Si@PEDOT composite electrode displays initial charge and discharge capacities of 2505 and 3349 mAh g–1 with a high coulombic efficiency of 74.8% during the first cycle. The capacity retention after 100 cycles was only 55.9%. Similarly, the Si@PThAA electrode exhibits initial charge-discharge capacities of 2284 and 4655 mAh g–1 with a very poor coulombic efficiency of 49.1%. Additionally, the capacity decayed to 43.5% after 100 cycles. The initial charge and discharge curves (Figure S10b) show that the Si@PThAA electrode exhibits a long sloping plateau between 1.8 and 0.2 V during the first discharge cycle. Additionally, the electrical resistance of PThAA (Table S1) is relatively high, which may contribute to the exceptionally high first-cycle discharge capacity. The poor electrical conductivity of PThAA may hinder electron transport, leading to excessive electrolyte decomposition and the formation of a thick SEI layer, which increases irreversible capacity. The interfacial resistance between Si and PThAA may also hinder lithium-ion extraction, resulting in lithium trapping within the electrode. In addition, specific functional groups in PThAA may interact with lithium ions, exacerbating lithium loss. These combined effects may explain the high irreversible capacity observed in the first cycle of the Si@PThAA electrode. Figure e(iii) shows that the Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA) electrode exhibits initial charge and discharge capacities of 2414 and 3142 mAh g–1, respectively, with a high coulombic efficiency of 76.83% during the first cycle. This high specific capacity of the Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA) electrode shows degradation in capacity retention as cycling increases, with discharge capacity remaining at 70.7% after 100 cycles. Furthermore, Figure e(iv) shows that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode exhibits a significant improvement in cycle-life performance compared to the Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, and Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA) electrodes. The results may be due to the P(EDOT-co-EBBT) shell increasing the electrical conductivity of Si nanoparticles and the PMAA brush acting as a binder. The binder is covalently bonded to the P(EDOT-co-EBBT), increasing the stability of the electrode.

In addition, the electrical resistance of the polymer bulks and the Si composite electrodes was evaluated using a 4-point probe instrument, as shown in Table S1. The results show that the electrical resistance of PEDOT, PThAA, P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA polymers is 0.8, 463.1, 48.0, and 68.7 ohms square–1, respectively. The higher electrical resistance of PThAA may be due to the steric hindrance of the substituent on the thiophene ring, resulting in less coplanarity of the backbone. Furthermore, the electrical resistance of the Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrodes is found to be 33.4, 55.6, 40.9, and 47.6 ohms square–1, respectively. The trend of electrical resistance for the electrodes is similar to that for the polymer bulks, suggesting that the shells of PEDOT-based polymers reduce the electrical resistance of the electrodes.

To investigate the surface morphology changes of the Si electrodes before and after cycling, SEM images were obtained. Figure a-d show SEM images of the as-prepared Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrodes. The SEM images of these composite electrodes show that the surface morphologies of the electrodes are very similar before cycling. However, the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrode exhibits a melt-like morphology, which is due to the unique morphology of the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush. After 100 cycles, Figure e-h show the SEM images of the Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrodes. All SEM images of Si electrodes after 100 cycles show that the particle size of the Si nanoparticles increases and the surface morphology of the Si nanoparticles after 100 cycles are similar. The increase in particle size may be due to the growth of the SEI layer, the aggregation of nanoparticles, structural reorganization, and volume expansion of silicon upon lithiation. Figure h shows that the morphology of the composite electrode with the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush exhibits melt-liked structure, which is due to that the polymer brush is covalently bond on the Si nanoparticles. Therefore, the polymer brush may enhance its mechanical properties of the electrode.

sec.head: Cycle-Life Performance of Cells with Different Binders
sec.p:
The cycle-life performance of cells with Si/PVDF, Si/PMAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, Si/P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA, and Si/(SBR/CMC) electrodes at a charge-discharge rate of 0.5 C is shown in Figure a(i), (ii), (iii), (iv) and (v), respectively. Figure a(i) shows that the conventional PVDF binder for carbon-based anodes exhibits poor cycle-life performance, with no energy capacity remaining after 50 cycles. When the binder is PMAA, the cycle-life performance is significantly enhanced as shown in Figure a(ii). This enhancement is due to the strong interaction between the binder and hydroxyl groups on Si via hydrogen bonding or the formation of a covalent bond of ester. Figure a(iii) shows that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush composite electrode has better cycle-life performance than the Si electrode with the PMAA binder. This improvement is likely because the conducting polymer P(EDOT-co-EBBT) enhances the electrical conductivity of the electrode. To study the importance of the PMAA brush on the Si electrode, the cycle-life performance of the Si electrode with free P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA polymer was also measured (Figure a(iv)). Figure a(iii) and (iv) show that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA electrode has better cycle-life performance than the Si/P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA electrode. This is due to the P(EDOT-co-EBBT) layer enhancing the electrical conductivity of Si nanoparticles and the PMAA brushes increasing the mechanical properties of the Si electrode, as confirmed by conductivity measurements and 180° peeling tests. Furthermore, Figure a(v) shows the cycle-life performance of a widely used binder (SBR/CMC) for Si anodes. The result indicates that the cycle-life performance of the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode is slightly better than that of the Si/(SBR/CMC) electrode.
Box 1 Moreover, to investigate the effects of different binders on electrode resistance

Moreover, to investigate the effects of different binders on electrode resistance, AC impedance technique was employed. The Nyquist plots of cells with Si/PVDF, Si/PMAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, and Si/free P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA electrodes are shown in Figure b, c, d, and e, respectively. The Nyquist plots include semicircles and a linear part, which contain the resistances of charge transfer, SEI, and diffusion reactions for the electrode. Figure S11 shows the Nyquist plots of experimental data and fitting data of the cells. An equivalent circuit was used to fit the plots, shown in Figure S12. The circular region observed in the Nyquist plots represents the impedance of charge transfer and SEI, while the linear portion at lower frequencies is associated with the diffusion of lithium ions in the bulk electrode. Table and Table Table summarizes Rs, RSEI, RCT, and Rtotal, representing the electrolyte resistance, solid-electrolyte interphase resistance, charge-transfer resistance, and total resistance, respectively, as obtained from the fitted equivalent circuit. After two charge-discharge cycles, the Rtotal of the cells with Si/PVDF, Si/PMAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, and Si/P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA electrode electrodes is 215.8, 133.7, 90.8, and 194.0 ohms, respectively. The cell with the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode exhibits the relatively low resistance. After 50 charge-discharge cycles, the Rs, RSEI, and RCT of the cells with Si/PVDF, Si/PMAA, and Si/free P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA electrode dramatically increase. However, the resistances for the cell with the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode do not obviously increase, which implies that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode is more stable. This trend remains consistent even after 100 cycles, indicating that the P(EDOT-co-EBBT) conducting polymer enhances electrical conductivity and that PMAA is covalently bonded to the Si nanoparticles.

sec.head: 180° Peeling Test and Lithium-Diffusion Coefficient of Si Composite Electrodes
sec.p: A 180° peeling test was used to evaluate the adhesion strength between the Cu foil and the Si composite electrodes. The peeling force-displacement curves of the Si composite electrodes are shown in Figure S8. Figure a shows the adhesion strength required to separate the Si composite made with different binders from the Cu foil. The adhesion force of the Si electrode with the PVDF binder is 16.2±7.7 N m–1. As reported in the literature, PVDF is not an appropriate binder for Si electrodes. For the PMAA binder, the adhesion force of the Si electrode is 142.2±10.1 N m–1, attributed to the strong interaction between the carboxyl groups of PMAA and the hydroxyl groups on the Si nanoparticles. This strong adhesion is one reason why the Si electrode with the PMAA binder exhibits improved cycle-life performance. Moreover, the adhesion force for the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode significantly rises to 168.5±20.5 N m–1. In contrast, the adhesion force for the Si/P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA is 53.3±13.7 N m–1. The enhanced performance of the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode could be due to the covalent attachment of the polymer brush served as a binder, which increases the mechanical property of the Si electrode.

The CV curves of Si composite electrodes with different binders were measured at various scan rates, as shown in Figure S13. The Randles-Sevcik equation was used to analyze the anodic peak current (Ip) at 0.49 V vs Li/Li+, and their linear relationship with the square root of the scan rates was employed to determine the diffusion coefficients of lithium ions (DLi+), as per the following equation:1E(RHE)=E(Ag/AgCl)+Eref+0.059pH2ECSA=Cdl/Cswhere Ip represents the peak current (A), F is Faraday’s constant (96,485 C mol−1), R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J K−1· mol−1), T is the absolute temperature (298 K), n the number of charge transfers, A is the area of the electrode surface (1.3273 cm2), D is the lithium diffusion coefficient, C is the concentration (mol cm−3) of lithium ion in the electrode, and v is scan rate (V s−1). Figure b shows that the slopes of the Ip-v1/2 plots are used to estimate the DLi+ of an electrode based on the Randles-Sevcik equation.

The Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode has a higher DLi+ of 1.35 × 10−11 cm2 s−1 compared with the Si/PVDF, Si/PMAA, and Si/P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA, which have DLi+ values of 2.85× 10−12, 5.46 × 10−12, and 7.92 × 10−12 cm2 s−1, respectively.
These results indicate that the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode has a high diffusion coefficient, leading to better electrochemical performance.

sec.head: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: In this study, we successfully synthesized and characterized a Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush as a binder-free anode material for lithium-ion batteries. To achieve this, the monomers EDOT and EBBT were copolymerized onto Si nanoparticles through oxidative polymerization, followed by the grafting of PMAA brushes onto the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT) core-shell nanoparticles via SI-ATRP, resulting in the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush structure. Several control Si-based electrodes, including Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, and Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), were also fabricated and evaluated. Characterizations of Si@PEDOT, Si@PThAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush nanoparticles were conducted using infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and scanning electron microscopy. Rheological studies on the electrode material slurries showed that strong interactions result from the PMAA brushes on Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA nanoparticles. The Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush anode exhitbited significantly improved C-rate and cycle-life performance of lithium-ion batteries. Compared with conventional binder-based systems, this binder-free anode showed enhanced adhesion strength and reduced resistance. Importantly, this core-shell configuration with grafted PMAA brushes substantially enhanced cell performance over the Si composite electrode with free P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA as a binder. This innovative design for the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush anode provides a promising pathway for advancing the performance and durability of next-generation lithium-ion batteries.

ack.p: The authors gratefully acknowledge support of the National Science and Technology Council in Taiwan through Grant NSTC 112-2113-M-110-012.

notes.head: Supporting Information
notes.p: The Supporting Information is available free of charge at https://pubs.acs.org

(1) Syntheses of PEDOT, PThAA, P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA; (2) NMR spectra; (3) Experimental data and fitting data for Niquist polts; (4) CV curves for Si composite electrodes with different binders at different scan rates; (5) Peeling force-displacement figures for Si electrodes; (6) Schematic representation of the peeling test setup; (7) Electrical resistance values for polymers and Si composite electrodes.
K.V and A.K contributed equally to this work

notes.head: Author Contributions
notes.p: Koustav and Avishek contributed equally to this work.

notes.head: Funding Sources
notes.p: This study was supported using internal funds from Emory University, a Biolocity pre-seed grant, and a Winship Cancer Institute Catalyst grant.

notes.head: Notes
notes.p: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

bio.p: Corresponding Author Jyh-Tsung Lee − Department of Chemistry and Center for Green-Energy Key Materials Research, National Sun Yat-sen University, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan; orcid.org/0000-0002-2658-4222;

caption.p: (a) IR spectra of (i) Si@PEDOT, (ii) Si@PThAA, (iii) Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and (iv) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush anodes. (b) IR spectra of (i) PEDOT, (ii) PThAA, (iii) P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and (iv) P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA.

TEM images of (a) Si, (b) Si@PEDOT, (c) Si@PThAA, (d) Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and (e) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush nanoparticles; SEM images of (f) Si, (g) Si@PEDOT, (h) Si@PThAA, (i) Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and (j) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush nanoparticles.
G’ and G” as functions of sweeping frequencies for (10 vol%) water-based electrode slurries of (a) Si@PEDOT, (b) Si@PThAA, (c) Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and (d) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush.
First five CV curves of (a) Si@PEDOT, (b) Si@PThAA, (c) Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and (d) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrodes.

C-rates performance of the cells with (a) Si@PEDOT, (b) Si@PThAA, (c) Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and (d) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrodes. (e) Cycle-life performance of the cells with (i) Si@PEDOT, (ii) Si@PThAA, (iii) Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and (iv) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrodes charged and discharged at 0.5 C.

SEM images of Si composites electrodes (a-d) before and (e-h) after 100 cycles. Si composite electrodes with (a)(e) Si@PEDOT, (b)(f) Si@PThAA, (c)(g) Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and (d)(h) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush.

(a) Cycle-life performance of cells with (i) Si/PVDF, (ii) Si/PMAA, (iii) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, (iv) Si/P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA, and (v) Si/(CMC/SBR) electrodes charged and discharged at 0.5 C. Nyquist plots of Li|1.0 M LiPF6-EC/DEC/FEC (45:45:10, w/w/w)|Si electrode with (b) PVDF, (c) PMAA, (d) P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush and (e) Si/free P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA binder at the 2nd, 50th, and 100th cycles.

(a) Stress force of a 180° peeling test of Si/PVDF, Si/PMAA, Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, and Si/P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA electrodes. and (b) Plots of CV oxidation and reduction peak currents vs the square root of scan rate for (i) the Si/PVDF, (ii) Si/PMAA, (iii) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush, and (iv) Si/P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA electrodes. The solid line represents the line of best fit to the data.

caption.title: Schematic illustration of synthesis of Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush anode.,
Syntheses of (a) Si@PEDOT, (b) Si@PThAA, (c) Si@P(EDOT-co-ThAA), and (d) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush anodes.

TEM images of (a) Si, (b) Si@PEDOT, (c) Si@PThAA, (d) Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and (e) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush nanoparticles; SEM images of (f) Si, (g) Si@PEDOT, (h) Si@PThAA, (i) Si@P(EDOT-co-PThAA), and (j) Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush nanoparticles.
Rs, RSEI, RCT, and Rtotal values for the coin cells with different Si composite electrodes after 2nd, 50th and 100th cycles obtained from EIS.

fn.label: a
fn.p: Furthermore, to understand the improved performance of the cell with the Si@P(EDOT-co-EBBT)-g-PMAA brush electrode.

fn.label: b
fn.p: The Si composite anodes were analyzed by CV using scan rates from 0.1 to 0.4.

fn.label: c
fn.p: The diffusion coefficients of lithium ions in Si composite electrodes, according to the Randles-Sevcik equation.

caption.title: Solubility parameters of the base oil, polar and non-polar portions of sulfonate detergent and Ca+2, and sulfonate.

row.entry: van der Waals contribution Solubility parameter ((J/cm3)0.5)
